turns.content: I'm onboarding to this codebase and I keep seeing anyhow::Result types everywhere. What's the actual difference between a raw anyhow::Error and an error that has been chained with context?

A raw anyhow::Error is just the failure itself, typically from a crate like this or std. When you chain it using anyhow::context, you wrap that original error inside a new error type that carries extra metadata about where it happened. The core trait implementation for this is impl Display and Debug on anyhow::Error, which lets the context format cleanly without breaking the existing debug output of the root cause.
So if I just return an error from a function, it will show the source file line number automatically?

Not automatically. The raw anyhow::Error only contains its own message and internal stack trace information. To get meaningful context like 'at /path/to/file.rs:42', you must explicitly wrap the error using anyhow::context. Without that wrapper, your logs will just say something like 'failed to parse' without telling you which function failed.

Got it, but I noticed my current code uses Result<T, std::io::Error> and I'm worried that mixing anyhow with standard errors will cause issues later. Is there a trait that forces everything to be compatible?

You don't need to mix them if you want consistency. The core pattern is to convert all downstream failures into anyhow::Result<T, E> where E is an anyhow::Error. This ensures that every error in your system shares the same context capabilities. You can use the standard library's io error by wrapping it with anyhow::anyhow!() or using std::convert::From trait implementations so you never have to manually convert types.
I see, but I thought anyhow errors couldn't be converted back to std errors easily. Can I still use standard error handling libraries if I switch?

Yes, you can convert them using the From trait or a custom adapter function because anyhow::Error implements the standard library's Error trait through the anyhow crate's internal design. This means your existing error handlers that expect std errors will work once you've normalized everything to anyhow.
Wait, I just realized something. If I wrap an error with context, won't it duplicate the message text? I saw a warning in my logs about double printing?

That's exactly why you use anyhow::context instead of simply wrapping strings manually. The wrapper function adds metadata without repeating the original error text. It preserves the root cause message while adding location context, so your logs show 'at /path/to/file.rs:42' followed by the original error message once.
Okay, but what if I need to pass this error through multiple layers? Will the context get lost?

If you chain errors properly using anyhow::context at each layer, the original message survives all the way down. You can call .chain() on an existing error to add more context without losing what came before it.
I'm confused about the difference between chaining and wrapping. Are they actually different approaches?

They are conceptually similar but serve different purposes. Chaining adds context to an existing error, while wrapping creates a new error type around another one using anyhow::context. Both preserve the original error's message and stack trace.
So if I have multiple failing steps in my pipeline, which approach should I use to keep track of where things went wrong?

Use chaining for sequential failures. If you're building a custom error type that aggregates multiple conditions, then wrapping with context makes sense. For most pipelines, chaining allows you to preserve the full stack trace and location info as errors bubble up.

Hey, I'm onboarding to this new service and I've got a working solution where errors bubble up. Right now if something fails in the database layer it just returns 'DatabaseError', but I'd like to know how to add more context so users can see exactly what went wrong without losing the root cause.

That's a common problem. The standard way is using anyhow's context builder pattern where you wrap errors with extra information before they propagate up the call stack. You use anyhow::context to prepend messages like 'Failed while processing request' or specific details about which operation failed.
Got it, so I'm wrapping my current error type with that context builder before returning it? Can you show me exactly how the chaining works in practice?

Sure. If your database error is already a anyhow::Result, you can call anyhow::context on it directly like this: anyhow::context("Failed to fetch user profile").err(). Then when that propagates up the chain, each layer can also add its own context using the same builder pattern.
Okay, but what if I'm not using anyhow and just have a plain Result or an enum error type? Does that still work?

You'll need to convert your existing error into an anyhow::Error first. That means implementing the Into trait for your custom types so they can be wrapped with context. Alternatively you could refactor those parts to use anyhow from the start since it makes this much easier.
I see, so I have a choice between refactoring or wrapping? What are the pros and cons of each approach in this situation?

Refactoring is cleaner long-term but costs more upfront work. Wrapping is faster to implement now but might become messy if you have many different error types that need context. For a new codebase like yours, I'd suggest wrapping first and refactor later once the pattern stabilizes.
Makes sense. One more thing though - what happens to the original error message when I chain contexts? Does it get lost?

It does not get lost at all. The context builder preserves the underlying error so you can still access it later using methods like .cause() or by inspecting the error chain. This way users see both the new context and the original failure reason.
That's really helpful. So I'll add context at each layer and keep the root cause intact? What if someone wants to debug this later?

Debugging becomes much easier because you can iterate through all the contexts in order plus the original error using anyhow's built-in utilities. You'll see a complete picture of how the failure propagated and where it started without having to parse logs manually.

I'm drowning in errors right now and I need to know what the actual difference between using a plain Result<T, E> and anyhow::Context<T, E>. My production server is spitting out generic 'file not found' messages when it should be telling me exactly which database query failed first.

That classic frustration happens when you lose the chain. A plain Result just wraps the error, so downstream code sees only the final failure without knowing what triggered it. anyhow::Context<T, E> solves this by wrapping an existing error with new context before propagating it, keeping the root cause visible while adding useful details like the query ID or user ID.
Exactly! I want to add the SQL statement and table name so developers can trace back immediately. But how do I chain this without rewriting my whole error handling logic everywhere?

You don't need to rewrite anything. You just call anyhow::Context::new() on your existing error, passing in the new details like the SQL statement and table name. This creates a new error that contains both the original failure and your added context, so when it bubbles up through the stack, every layer sees the full story.
Got it, but can I still use standard Result patterns after wrapping with Context? Does anyhow::Context work seamlessly with match statements or if-let?

Yes, it works perfectly. Once you wrap the error using anyhow::Context, the resulting type is still an implementation of std::error::Error and implements Display and Debug like any other Result type. You can match on it with standard patterns or use if-let without changing your existing logic.
Perfect, so I'll add the SQL context to my database query error before returning it from the function. What about logging? Should I log the original error separately too?

You should log both. Log the full anyhow::Error object that includes your Context wrapper, and optionally also log just the root cause if you want to separate concerns. The context will contain all the details you added, so logging it once gives you everything you need.
One last thing - what happens if I accidentally drop the Context wrapper before propagating? Will I lose the chain?

If you unwrap or clone it incorrectly, yes you can lose the context. Always pass the anyhow::Error object through your functions without unwrapping until it reaches the final error handler. That way the entire chain remains intact and every caller sees the full picture.
Thanks! I'll refactor my database layer to use anyhow::Context and add the SQL context before each return. This should fix our messy error logs immediately.
You're welcome. That's exactly the right approach. With proper context chaining, your error messages will tell the complete story from first cause to final failure, making debugging a breeze.

I'm trying to build a new deployment tool in Rust but I keep hitting walls when errors bubble up from my worker threads. My current approach just propagates simple String messages, and by the time they reach the CLI, users have no idea what actually failed or why. I've heard about using anyhow for this but I don't understand how to chain context properly versus just wrapping values.

The core issue is that you're likely treating errors as opaque strings instead of structured data objects. When you use std::error::Error, you need to ensure every error implements the standard trait correctly so it can be chained. However, for your specific case where you want to add metadata like timestamps or user IDs without manually implementing traits, anyhow is much better because it automatically handles Error impls and context attachment. The key difference is that with plain strings or basic Result types, you lose the ability to inspect the original cause later during debugging.
Okay so I should stop using simple Strings and switch to anyhow::Result instead? But what exactly does the context field do in those errors?

Exactly, switch from String or plain Result<T,E> to anyhow::Result<T,E>. The context field is a named metadata attachment that you add when wrapping an error with .context(). For example, if your worker function returns Err with status code 503, you can call .context("Failed to deploy application") before returning it. This creates a new Error instance where the original cause (the 503) is preserved as context data, allowing downstream callers to distinguish between 'application failed' and 'database connection refused'. You don't need to manually implement std::error::Error unless you're building custom types.

That makes sense but I'm worried about performance. If every call site adds a .context() string, won't that accumulate overhead and make the error messages huge? Is there a way to avoid repeating context strings?

You are correct to worry about redundancy, but you don't need to repeat the same context multiple times if it's already attached. The context field stores only metadata like timestamps or resource names, not the full error chain text. If your deployment tool has a shared logger module that attaches "deployment started" at the top level, then when individual worker errors propagate up, they can just call .context("Worker failed") without duplicating the parent context. Actually, you should avoid calling .context() on already-contextualized errors unless you need to add specific new metadata; otherwise, the error chain grows unnecessarily large.
I see. But what if I want to manually build a custom error type that combines anyhow with my own logic? Can I still use any of those features?

Yes, you can absolutely combine custom types with anyhow by implementing the required trait for your custom struct. When your custom error implements std::error::Error and uses anyhow internally, you get both flexibility and automatic context handling. For instance, you could define a DeploymentError struct that wraps anyhow::Result and adds fields like deployment_id or environment name directly in the struct definition. Then when you call .context() on it, anyhow will automatically invoke your custom Error impl to format the message while preserving all internal data.
So I should refactor my code now? Like replace all my Result<_, String> returns with anyhow::Result and add context calls?

Yes, but do it incrementally. Start by identifying functions that currently return plain Strings or basic errors where you know the caller needs more context. Replace those specific return types with anyhow::Result<T,E>. Then in those same locations, insert .context() calls before returning Err instances. You don't need to rewrite your entire tool at once; focus on the hot paths where debugging is hardest first. This gradual migration reduces risk while giving you immediate improvements in error visibility.
One last thing though - what happens if someone catches an anyhow error and tries to match on it without using any specific pattern? Will they just get a generic message?

If you don't use the .unwrap() or .expect() methods, you can still inspect the underlying cause through .cause(). This returns Option<&E> where E is your original error type. So even if someone catches an anyhow::Error without knowing it's contextualized, they can call .cause() to retrieve the first wrapped error and examine its message directly. The context fields remain attached but aren't automatically exposed in basic pattern matching unless you explicitly access them via methods like .context(). However, this is rarely needed since most callers will just want to display the full chain including metadata.
